name: Nyamwezi
description: The Nyamwezi ('People of the Moon') are the dominant ethnic group of Tanzania's central plateau, historically the greatest long-distance traders in East Africa. Their trading caravans connected the interior copper and ivory-producing regions to the Swahili coast at Bagamoyo and Zanzibar, covering thousands of kilometres on foot. Under Chief Mirambo (c.1840–84), they built a powerful military federation that rivalled the Arab trading empire of Tippu Tip and briefly threatened Arab commercial dominance in East Africa.
cultural_highlights: Long-distance caravan trade — Nyamwezi porters and traders dominated the 19th-century interior-to-coast trade in ivory, copper, and slaves
Chief Mirambo's federation — 1860s–1884 military state that challenged Arab commercial dominance and impressed European explorers including H.M. Stanley
Ntemi chieftaincy system — village chiefs (ntemi) governed through consensus councils; the same system used by the Sukuma and related peoples
Vigango memorial posts — carved wooden posts erected in honour of deceased important community members

food_traditions.name: Ugali and dagaa
food_traditions.description: Maize ugali with dried lake sardines — the central plateau staple shared across Tanzania

food_traditions.name: Kisamvu (cassava leaf stew)
food_traditions.description: Pounded cassava leaves cooked with groundnut paste and coconut milk

food_traditions.name: Roasted maize (mahindi ya kuchoma)
food_traditions.description: Roadside roasted sweetcorn — a caravan-era travel food tradition that persists in modern Tanzania

music_traditions: Ngoma ya uganga — healing drum ceremonies led by spirit medium practitioners to treat illness and resolve conflicts
Beni ngoma — a competitive military-style ngoma with uniforms and mock-military choreography; a colonial-era adaptation of Nyamwezi warrior culture
Litungu lyre — a 6-8 string box lyre used in personal music-making and small social gatherings
